coredata.dc:title: Tuning the electronic and magnetic properties of triangular boron nitride quantum dots via carbon doping
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xi, Yan
Zhao, Xiaoyang
Wang, Aizhu
Wang, Xiaopeng
Bu, Hongxia
Zhao, Mingwen
coredata.dc:description: The energetic stability, electronic and magnetic properties of carbon-doped triangular boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) were investigated using first principle calculation within density functional theory (DFT). Different edge structures, doping positions, and carbon concentrations are considered. We found that the substitutional C atom energetically prefers to reside in the minority sublattice of the BNQDs, and the planner structures of the BNQDs are well preserved. When the carbon dopant moves from the inner to the outer region of the BNQDs, the HOMO–LUMO gap decreases in an oscillating way, which is even smaller than that of graphene quantum dots. After carbon doping, BNQDs have non-zero magnetic moment ground states. There is an impurity state above or below the Fermi level of C-doped BNQDs, depending on substituting majority or minority sublattice. This offers a promising way of tuning the electronic and magnetic properties of BNQDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Two carbon-doping in triangular N-edged BNQD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic structure of a zigzag triangular BNQD with N edged, containing 82 atoms, wherein the red labels indicate the different doping positions. (b) left and right are total spin polarized density of states corresponding to pristine Tri-NH–BN QD and Tri-BH–BN QD, respectively. Red and blue dotted curves express spin up and spin down. Dash line is the Fermi level. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The formation energy sketch of one carbon atom doping BNQDs relative to pristine one.

Left panel: Electronic Kohn–Sham energy levels of some C-doped BNQDs in the region near the Fermi level on left. Solid (or dot) lines indicate occupied (or unoccupied) states. The right side corresponds to its total and partial spin polarized density of states. Red and blue lines represent the energy level of spin-up and spin-down branches, respectively. Dash line is the Fermi level. Right panel: spatial distribution of spin density of the configuration given above. Yellow and blue bubbles represent the isosurfaces of spin-up and spin-down density. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The same physical pictures as the Fig.3 describe two carbon atoms doped BNQDs with spin polarized.
The image displays two differ size and geometry corresponding to B9CN12H12, B54CN63H30, B15CN14H14, and B26CN27H18, respectively.

Total and partial spin polarized density of states corresponding to B9CN12H12, B27CN33H21, B54CN63H30, B26CN27H18, and B15CN14H14, respectively. Red and blue lines represent the energy level of spin-up and spin-down branches. Dash line is the Fermi level, the Fermi level is set to zero. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Formation energy, magnetization and HUMO–LUMO energy gap of various C-doped position BNQDs with differ edged.
Formation energy, magnetization and HUMO–LUMO energy gap of various two carbon atoms doped position BNQDs with differ edged.
Total charge transformation for the CB1Nj (j=4–6) configuration. The N or B column corresponding to adjacent atoms for C substitute for B1 site and Nj (j=4–6) sites, respectively.
Formation energy, magnetization and HUMO–LUMO energy gap of the most stable configuration CB7 for various size and geometry structure.

head.ce:title: Tuning the electronic and magnetic properties of triangular boron nitride quantum dots via carbon doping

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The energetic stability, electronic and magnetic properties of carbon-doped triangular boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) were investigated using first principle calculation within density functional theory (DFT). Different edge structures, doping positions, and carbon concentrations are considered. We found that the substitutional C atom energetically prefers to reside in the minority sublattice of the BNQDs, and the planner structures of the BNQDs are well preserved. When the carbon dopant moves from the inner to the outer region of the BNQDs, the HOMO–LUMO gap decreases in an oscillating way, which is even smaller than that of graphene quantum dots. After carbon doping, BNQDs have non-zero magnetic moment ground states. There is an impurity state above or below the Fermi level of C-doped BNQDs, depending on substituting majority or minority sublattice. This offers a promising way of tuning the electronic and magnetic properties of BNQDs.

► The research includes stability, electronic and magnetic properties. ► The substitutional C atom energetically prefers to reside in minority sublattice. ► When the C dopant moves outside, the HOMO–LUMO gap decreases in an oscillating way. ► Carbon-doped BNQDs have non-zero magnetic moment ground states. ► The C-doped BNQDs characteristic is size- and geometry independence.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanostructures with honeycomb-like network have been intensively investigated over the past two decades due to their unique structures, fascinating properties and promising potential applications in various fields. Most of these works focus on carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Tuning the electronic properties is quite necessary to achieve the application of these materials in nanoscaled electronic devices. Boron and nitrogen which are the neighbors of carbon in periodic table have been used as dopants to reach this goal. Nitrogen (or boron) atom has one more (or less) electron than carbon atom and can act as donor (or accepter) as they are doped into carbon materials. This feature has been revealed in various carbon nanostructures, such as CNTs [1–12] and GNRs [13–21]. More interestingly, BNC hybrid nanostructures are predicted to possess intriguingly properties depending on elemental composition and the atomic species arrangements [22–24], which offers more freedoms for the electronic structure modifications of these materials. For example, theoretical investigations of several B x C y N z layered structures indicate that the structures with large band gap always have a B/N ratio of one [25]. The hybrid BN–CNT may undergo nonmagnetic semiconductor—spin-polarized metal–nonmagnetic semiconductor transition and exhibit intrinsic half-metallicity by controlling the ratio of BN component [26]. Interface states of BN/C nanotube heterostructures lead to charge redistribution, resulting in the formation of a build-in electric field and the modulation of the static electric potential profiles in the heterostructures [27].

Additionally, the BN analog of graphene (refereed to as graphene-like BN nanosheet hereafter) has been synthesized via different approaches [28–33]. Analogously, BN nanostructures with honeycomb-like network, such as nanoribbons and quantum dots can be produced from this two-dimensional nanosheet [34–36]. However, due to the iconicity of the B–N bonds, the electronic properties of these BN nanostructures differ significantly from their carbon counterparts. For example, Son et al. demonstrated that H-terminated GNRs with either armchair or zigzag shaped edges have a nonzero and finite energy gap [37–39]. The zigzag graphene nanoribbons (ZGNRs) have special localized edge states, which are antiferromagnetically coupled to each other [40–43]. The ZGNRs may emerge half-metallicity through adding a external field or edge modification [44,45]. However, neither the localized spin-polarized edge states nor half-metallicity were found in the boron nitride nanoribbons (BNNRs) with the edge atoms being passivated by hydrogen atoms [35]. Band gap oscillates in armchair BNNRs, but decreases monotonically with the increase of the ribbon width for zigzag BNNRs [46]. Other works have also found that the fully bare zigzag BNNRs and an H-terminated B edge are half-metals, which can be attributed to the localized ferromagnetic edge states at the bare N edge of the ZBNNRs [47,48]. If the edge atoms are passivated by hydrogen atoms, the half-metallicity disappears. First-principles calculations also indicate that controlling the hydrogenation ratio can precisely achieve semiconductor–half-metal–metal transition in zigzag BNNRs [49]. Additionally, the spin polarization of graphene QDs (GQDs) depends on their topological properties. For example, the triangular GQDs with zigzag edges have degenerated zero-energy states, quite large relaxation time and metallic ferromagnetism [50–53]. The net magnetic moments in triangular GQDs satisfy a so-called Lieb's theorem [54]. However, owing to the different valence electrons and on-site energies of B and N atoms, the fully hydrogenated boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) have spin-unpolarized ground states, in contrast to the graphene counterparts [36].

Tuning the electronic properties of these BN nanostructures is crucial for their application in building nanoscaled electronic devices. Carbon atom is an ideal doping element due to the reasons mentioned above. The rule of carbon in modify the electronic structures of BNQDs would differ significantly from that of boron or nitrogen in GQDs, because of the big difference of the binding characters and the edge structures between BNQDs and GQDs. However, in contrast to the abundant theatrical efforts devoted to the BN-doped carbon nanomaterials, few studies have been reported on C-doped BN nanostructures. This becomes the goal of the present work.

Here, through first-principles calculations within density functional theory (DFT), we reported on the geometric stability, electronic and magnetic properties C-doped BNQDs with triangular shape and hydrogen-passivated zigzag edges. We focus on the effects of doping position and carbon concentration on these properties. We found that the substitutional C atom energetically prefers to reside in the minority sublattice of the BNQDs, and the planner structures of the BNQDs are well preserved. When the carbon dopant moves from the inner to the outer region of the BNQDs, the HOMO–LUMO gap decreases in an oscillating way, which is even smaller than that of graphene quantum dots. After carbon doping, BNQDs have non-zero magnetic moment ground states. The predicted behaviors provide opportunities for spintronic devices designing with C doping BNQDs.

We performed first-principle calculations using the plane wave basis Vienna ab initio simulation package known as VASP code [55,56], implementing the spin-polarized density functional theory (DFT) and projector augmented wave approach (PAW) pseudopotentials [57,58]. The electron-correlation interaction was treated within the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the Perdew–Wang (PW91) exchange-correlation functional [59]. We also adopted a local density approximation (LDA) and PBE for the exchange-correlation functional and found that the results obtained from GGA, PBE, and LDA calculations are very similar. The QDs were placed in a large superpcell with vacuum region of 18Å to exclude the mirror interactions between QDs. Only gamma point was considered in the Brillouin zone integrations. The coordinates of the atoms were fully optimized using conjugate gradient (CG) procedure until the maximal Hellmam–Feynman forces acting on them were smaller the 0.02eV/Å. The energy convergence was chosen as 10−4. In the spin-polarization calculations, different initial spin configurations were adopted to obtain the magnetic moment of the ground states.

To evaluate the plausibility of BNQDs, we defined the formation energies (E form) under different environments as follows:

was calculated from nitrogen molecular in gas phase, while under B-rich conditions, a metallic α–β boron phase was used as a reservoir. In both cases

was calculated from graphene system.

Our calculations focus on two selected boron nitride quantum dots (BNQDs) (B28N33H21 and B33N28H21) with triangular morphology. The B or N atoms at the zigzag edges are passivated by hydrogen atoms to increase the stability the systems [36]. Structural optimization indicates that the atoms distort slightly along the direction perpendicular to the basal plane for the B-edged BNQDs, while the N-edged BNNQ displays higher planar stability. In contrast to the electron spin-polarization of the carbon counterparts, both N-edged and B-edged BNQDs have spin-unpolarized ground states, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of these two BNQDs are 4.35eV and 4.89eV, respectively. These results are in good agreement with the previous work [36].

We then considered 28 doping configurations of the two triangular BNQDs doped with a single carbon atom. The doping sites are indicated in Fig. 1(a). Hereafter, we labeled the doping configuration of a carbon atom in the i-th B site (or N site) of the N-edged BNQD (B28N33H21) as CBi (or CNi ) whereas those of the B-edged BNQD (B33N28H21) as BCBi (or BCNi) (i=1–7). Structural optimization indicates the C–B and C–N bond lengths of N-edged BNQDs was stretched more than 10% regardless of doping sites, while in the case of B-edged BNQDs, the C–B bond shrinks less than 9% and the C–N bond elongates about 1.3%, compared to the B–N bond length of 1.52Å in the undoped BNQDs, respectively. Such difference may be related to the different electronic structures between B-edged and N-edged BNQDs. These carbon-doping BNQDs display good planar stability with deformation along the orientation perpendicular the basal plane less than 2.0%.

To test the plausibility of C-doped BNQDs, we calculate the formation energies using first-principles calculations and list them in Table 1. The dependence of the formation energy on the doping site is also plotted in Fig. 2. From these results we can see the following features: (1) carbon dopant in both CBi (i=1–7) and BCNi (i=2–7) configurations prefers to reside in the inner area under ambient surroundings (B-rich or N-rich). This is in contrast to the case of the B/N doped GQDs where the dopant prefers to lie in the edge than that the inner region [22]. While for the CNi and BCBi (i=1–7) systems, the dopants favor the edge positions. (2) The CNi and BCNi dopping configurations possess better stability in B-rich conditions than in N-rich conditions, while under N-rich environment, CBi and BCBi become energetically preferable. In both cases, C atom favors to substitute the B (or N) atom in the minority sublattice of BNQDs. (3) CBi is energetically the most favorable among the doping configurations considered in this work under N-rich environment. We also compare the formation energies of C-doped BNQDs with those of the B- or N-doped GQDs. It is found that the former is only half of the later, suggesting that doping BNQDs with C is energetically more favorable than doping GQDs with N or B. This provides an available way to control the electronic structure as well as the electronic distribution for triangular BNQD with zigzag edges, which is important in designing hexagonal boron nitride based electronic devices.

The energy gap (LOMO–LUMO gap) between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) of the C-doped BNQDs calculated from the present calculations are listed in Table 1. Differnet from the pristine BNQDs, these C-doped BNQDs have narrow HOMO–LUMO gaps even comparable to GDQs. When the dopant moves from the edge positions to the interior, the HOMO–LUMO gap decreases in an oscillating way. The BNQDs with C substituting for N sites have a larger energy gap than that of the BNQDs with C substituting for B sites. Although the DFT calculations within generalized gradient approximation (GGA) always underestimate the energy gap, the variational trend revealed in this work is expected to be reasonable.

We calculated the electronic structures and spin-polarization of the C-doped BNQDs using first-principles calculations. The result indicates that the ground states of C-doped BNQDs have non-zero magnetic moment, similar to cases of C doped BN nanoribbons and nanotubes [46,60–63]. Carbon atom has one less (or more) electron than B (or N) atom. Therefore, when a B (or N) atoms was substituted by a carbon atom, an electron (or hole) was introduced into relevant structures, accompanied by the formation of defect levels within the HOMO–LUMO gap of BNQDs. Fig. 3 presents different spin-resolved Kohn–Sham energy levels near the Fermi level and total and partial spin polarized density of states of C-doped BNQDs. Clearly, C substitution for B moves the Fermi level to a high energy, while of the substitution of N moves the Fermi level downward somewhat. For example, the spin-up branch of CB7 has a HOMO–LUMO gap of 0.71eV, while the spin-down branch 4.40eV. The Fermi level for all the B substituted structures is close to the LUMO while in the case of N substituted its locates near the HOMO. The highest occupied impurity states mainly come from spin-up and spin-down branch contribution for the B and N substituted structures, respectively. While the number of Pz electrons in C-doped BNQDs becomes odd because the C dopant introduces one more Pz electron to the system. The electron density of states (PDOS) projected onto C atoms indicates that the energy levels near the Fermi level are contributed by the C 2pz orbital of the C atom. This is also consistent with the spin density distribution as shown in Fig.3. In all the C-doped BNQDs, the C doping position has no obviously effect on impurity levels location.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two carbon-doping in triangular N-edged BNQD
ce:section.ce:para: The N-edged BNQDs doped with two carbon atoms (2C-doped BNQDs) were then calculated using first-principles calculations. We first fixed one C atom in the edged position B1 or N2, then changed the doping position of the second C atom, resulting in 26 differ configurations in all. These configurations are denoted as CBiαj and CNiαj (α=B/N, i=1 and 2, j=1–7). Structure optimization indicates the variation for the C–N bond length is less than 4%, and the C–B bond length were enlarged about 10% and B–N bond length enlarged less than 7.9%, compared with the pristine B–N bond length. Additionally, the 2C-doped BNQDs appear the same beautiful planar stability as one carbon-doped case.

We then evaluate the stability of 2C-doped structures through calculating the formation energy as shown in Table 2 and Fig. 2. Table 2 gives the formation energies relative to pristine N-edged BNQDs under B- or N-rich circumstance, respectively. The variation of relative formation energy as a function of doping position is shown in Fig. 2. The following features can be seen from Fig. 2. (1) CB1Bj configurations under N-rich environment are energetically most favorable among all the 2C-doped systems, whereas the CN2Nj structures are most unstable. (2) The distribution of 2C-doped states lean to locate in the edged B and N sites rather than the inner regions, especially the adjacent sites forming a C–C chain. (3) The difference of formation energy value between B-rich and N-rich condition for all the 2C-doped structures is neglectable. The C-doped position N5 emerges specific characteristic which will be explained at the terminal of this portion.

The electronic properties for 2C-doped BNQDs have been calculated, and the calculated HOMO-LUMO gap for different configurations are listed in Table 2, from which we find that the energy gaps of the 2C-doped BNQDs vary from 0.12eV to 3.88eV, which are smaller than that of pristine BNQD (4.35eV). Furthermore, the energy gap depends on the 2C-doped positions. When the two C atoms are in the same sublatttice, the HOMO-LUMO gaps are smaller than those with the two C atoms in different sublattices. For example, the CB1N1 has the largest HOMO-LUMO gap (3.88eV), and the CN1N4 has the smallest one (0.12eV). The latter value is even narrower than the pristine GQDs energy gap 0.59eV [22].

When one extra electron or hole is added in BNQDs, they will have spin polarized ground states with the net spin of about 1/2. Identically, if two electrons or holes are introduced, the total net spin will be 1 or 0. Therefore, when two B or N atoms of BNQDs are replaced by two C atoms, the net spin of the systems will be 1 or 0. However, our results are different from above as listed in Table 2, the magnetization for CB1Bj (j=1–3, 5, 6) and CN2Nj (j=1–3) decreases to zero. The spin-resolved Kohn-Sham energy levels near the Fermi level for the spin polarized configuration for 2C-doped BNQDs are shown in Fig. 4. Clearly, the electronic structures are sensitive to the doping position. The Fermi level for CB1Bj (j=4, 7) moves upward to LUMO and in the case of CN2Nj (j=4–7) it moves downward to HOMO compared pristine BNQDs. There are two doping levels near the Fermi level. The 2C-doped position plays an important role in impurity level location, for example, the doping state for CB1Bj (j=4, 7) lie below Fermi level whereas for CN2Nj (j=4–7) locates above Fermi level. From the total and partial spin polarized density of states in Fig. 4, we can see that the spin density of the 2C-doped BNQDs mainly come from the spin-polarized 2Pz orbital of C atoms. The explanation for partially depolarization for CB1Bj (j=1–3, 5, 6) and CN2Nj (j=1–3) should be the larger charge transition between two closer edged C atoms. The spin polarized calculation indicates the lower energy states are zero magnetization.

The CN5 configuration has particular spin polarized density of states and spatial distribution of spin density sketch as shown in Fig. 4. Interestingly, CB1N5 also displays magnetic ground states, although it has the same electrons as the undoped BNQD. The CB1N5 occupied energy states of spin up and spin down branch emerges asymmetry compared with CB1N4 and CB1N6. This symmetry breaking comes from not only the carbon atoms but also the B and N atoms adjacent to dopants. For further interpretation, we calculated the charge transition around the doping position as shown in Table 3. From it we can see that the charge transition between adjacent B and C atom in the CN5 configuration is not as remarkable as that in CN4 and CN6 configurations. This may be the reason why the CN5 configuration has special spin-polarized ground state.

The last part of the article describes how the size and geometry affects the properties of the C-doped BN quantum dots. We selected the most stable C-doped configuration CB7 to investigate. We performed B9CN12H12 and B54CN63H30 with triangular morphology in Fig. 5 (a, b) to check the size-dependence. In order to research the geometry effect, we considered hexagon and rhombus structures as shown in Fig. 5(c, d). From Table 4., we can found that the formation energy and HOMO-LOMO gap rely on size and geometry, which is attributed to BNQDs size- and composition-dependence rather than C-doped influence. However, size and geometry has no effect on the magnetic feature of C-doped triangular BN quantum dots. The total and partial spin polarized density of states in Fig. 6 indicates that the Fermi level and impurity state position is independent of the two factors.

First-principles calculations on the triangular B/N-edge BNQDs doped with C atoms show that the stability, energy gap and total magnetic moment depend on both the doping position and the doping concentration. When one C atom replaces a B or N atom of BNQDs, it prefers to take place in inner region of the minority sublattice. For the 2C-doped structures, the C atoms prefer to reside in the edge of BNQDs. When a carbon atom is doped into BNQDs, the ground state of the systems is spin-polarized regardless of doping position and the BNQDs edge type. While in the cases of 2C-doped BNQDs spin-polarization occurs only when the carbon substitution occurs in the inner region. There is an impurity state above or below the Fermi level of C-doped BNQDs, depending on substituting majority or minority sublattice. The properties of C-doped BNQDs are independent of the exchange-correlation function, size and geometry structure. This offers a promising way of tuning the electronic and magnetic properties of BNQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Basic Research Program of China (No. 2012CB932302), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 10974119), and the Natural Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars of Shandong Province (No. JQ201001).
